text.title: Rate Limiting Anti-patterns
text.summary: In-process counters, proxy IP misuse, missing Retry-After, and clock skew errors.

antipattern.description: In-process dict or lru_cache counters for rate limiting in multi-replica deployments.
antipattern.reason: Each replica allows its own full quota; a client hitting N replicas gets N times the intended limit.

antipattern.description: Keying the rate limiter on request.client.host which returns 127.0.0.1 behind a reverse proxy.
antipattern.reason: Every client appears as the same IP; the first caller trips the global limit, blocking all users simultaneously.
Well-behaved clients without Retry-After retry immediately in tight loops, amplifying load during the overload event.

antipattern.description: Sliding-window test that asserts count < limit instead of count <= limit.
antipattern.reason: Off-by-one allows one extra request beyond the stated limit; boundary tests must include the exact-limit case.

antipattern.description: Using time.time() on app servers for sliding-window timestamps instead of Redis TIME command.
antipattern.reason: Servers' clocks drift by seconds; requests get counted in wrong windows, allowing bursts at clock-divergence boundaries.

antipattern.description: Allowing the agent to finalize tier limit values (100/hour, 1000/hour) without human approval.
antipattern.reason: Tier limits are pricing and trust decisions; wrong values create revenue impact or DoS exposure that is hard to walk back.

rule.statement: Separate abuse limits (low, IP-based, short window, returns 429) from quota limits (high, key-based, long window, returns 402 or custom) and document the choice per route.
rule.rationale: Conflating abuse protection with billing quotas causes either over-restriction of legitimate users or inadequate abuse prevention.

text.reference: k6 script to verify limit headers and 429 boundary behavior
